title: Options & Hedging
description: Payoffs, parity and Greeks — where EV, distributions and market making all converge.

levels.title: Payoffs
intro: A CALL is the right (not obligation) to BUY at strike K; a PUT is the right to SELL at K. At expiry: call pays max(S − K, 0), put pays max(K − S, 0).
Options are bets on the distribution of S (Unit 3!) — their fair value is just E[payoff], discounted.

exercises.prompt: A call option gives its owner:
options: The obligation to buy at the strike
The right to buy at the strike
The right to sell at the strike
exercises.explanation: Right, never obligation — that asymmetry is the whole point. You exercise only when it's profitable, so the payoff is floored at zero (and the option itself always costs something).

exercises.prompt: You hold a call with strike 100. At expiry the stock is at 112. Payoff per share?
exercises.explanation: max(112 − 100, 0) = 12: buy at 100 via the option, sell at the market's 112. If the stock had expired at 95, payoff = 0 — not −5.

exercises.prompt: You hold a put with strike 50. The stock expires at 44. Payoff per share?
exercises.explanation: max(50 − 44, 0) = 6: buy in the market at 44, sell at 50 through the option. Puts profit when prices fall — built-in insurance.

exercises.prompt: Buy a call and sell a put, same strike and expiry. Your combined expiry payoff looks like:
options: Owning the stock (bought at the strike) — a synthetic forward
exercises.explanation: Above K the call pays S−K; below K the short put costs S−K (negative). Either way you get S − K: identical to a forward at K. Replication like this is the engine of all option pricing.

exercises.prompt: Maximum possible LOSS from buying a call option?
exercises.explanation: Worst case you let it expire worthless and lose only the premium. Selling (writing) a naked call is the opposite story: capped gain, unlimited loss.

levels.title: Parity & Pricing Logic
intro: Put-call parity: C − P = S − K·e^(−rT). It's pure no-arbitrage (Unit 4) — the synthetic forward you just built must price like a real one, or free money exists.
With r = 0 it's simply C − P = S − K. Given any three of C, P, S, K you can price the fourth.

exercises.explanation: Long call + short put = synthetic forward = S − K in value. If the equation breaks, an arbitrageur sells the expensive side, buys the cheap side, and locks the difference.

exercises.prompt: Zero rates. Stock at 100, strike 100, and the put trades at 3. Fair price of the call?
exercises.explanation: C = P + S − K = 3 + 100 − 100 = 3. At-the-money (with no rates/dividends), calls and puts are worth the same — the distribution of gains mirrors losses.

exercises.prompt: Zero rates. Stock at 105, strike 100, put trades at 2. Fair call price?
exercises.explanation: C = P + S − K = 2 + 105 − 100 = 7. Decompose: 5 of intrinsic value (S−K) + 2 of time value, matching the put's pure time value. Parity keeps them honest.

exercises.prompt: Volatility rises (everything else fixed). Option prices:
exercises.explanation: Payoffs are floored at zero, so a wider distribution (Unit 3) adds upside without adding downside — for calls AND puts. Options are fundamentally long volatility; that's why traders quote them in vol.

exercises.prompt: An American option (exercisable anytime) versus an otherwise identical European one (expiry only) is worth:
options: Less — more decisions to mess up
At least as much — extra rights can't have negative value
exercises.explanation: A superset of rights can never be worth less — you can always ignore the extra right. (Fun wrinkle: for calls on non-dividend stocks, early exercise is never optimal, so they're worth exactly the same.)

levels.title: Hedging & the Greeks
intro: Greeks measure sensitivities. DELTA: how much the option price moves per $1 of stock move — and the hedge ratio. GAMMA: how fast delta itself changes. THETA: time decay.
Delta hedging is Unit 4's inventory management applied to options: stay flat the direction, trade the volatility.

options: Price sensitivity to a $1 move in the underlying
exercises.explanation: A 0.4-delta call gains ≈ $0.40 when the stock rises $1. Bonus intuition: delta roughly approximates the probability the option expires in the money.

exercises.prompt: You own 100 calls (each on one share) with delta 0.4. How many shares do you SHORT to be delta-neutral?
exercises.explanation: 100 × 0.4 = 40 shares of stock-equivalent exposure; short 40 to flatten it. Now small stock moves don't hurt you — you've isolated the volatility bet.

exercises.prompt: Why must a delta-hedged position be re-hedged as the stock moves?
options: Gamma: delta itself changes as the spot moves, unbalancing the hedge
It doesn't — one hedge is permanent
exercises.explanation: Delta is a local slope; gamma is its curvature. Long options + delta hedging means you systematically buy low / sell high as you re-hedge — that 'gamma scalping' income is what pays for theta.

exercises.prompt: A straddle (buy call + put, both strike 100) costs $8 total. What stock price is the UPPER breakeven at expiry?
exercises.explanation: You profit if the move exceeds the premium: breakevens at 100 ± 8 → 92 and 108. A straddle is a pure bet that realized volatility beats the vol you paid (Unit 3 meets Unit 5's sizing!).

options: Options gain value as expiry nears
A long option position bleeds value each day, all else equal
exercises.explanation: Less time = less chance for a big move = less optionality value. Long options pay theta to own gamma; short options collect theta but carry the tail risk. Every option book is that one trade-off. Congratulations — you've completed QuantQuest! 🎓
